questions.text: Which of the following are storage areas that the CPU can access directly? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: According to the slides, the CPU can only access main memory and registers directly. Cache is also mentioned as sitting between them for performance, while secondary storage requires I/O operations.

questions.text: A pair of **base** and **limit** registers define which of the following?

choices.label: The total capacity of physical RAM
choices.text: The total capacity of physical RAM

choices.label: The logical address space available to a process
choices.text: The logical address space available to a process

choices.label: The size of the swap file on the backing store
choices.text: The size of the swap file on the backing store

choices.label: The speed of memory access cycles
choices.text: The speed of memory access cycles

questions.explanation: The base and limit registers define the range of logical addresses a process can legally access, effectively defining its logical address space.

questions.text: At which stage of address binding is the process capable of being moved during execution from one memory segment to another?
questions.explanation: Execution time binding allows binding to be delayed until run time, enabling the process to move between segments during execution.

questions.text: What is the primary function of the **Memory-Management Unit (MMU)**?

choices.label: To map virtual addresses to physical addresses at run time
choices.text: To map virtual addresses to physical addresses at run time

choices.label: To allocate CPU time to various processes
choices.text: To allocate CPU time to various processes

choices.label: To perform garbage collection in main memory
choices.text: To perform garbage collection in main memory

questions.explanation: The MMU is a hardware device that maps virtual (logical) addresses generated by the CPU to physical addresses in memory.

questions.text: Which memory management technique ensures that a routine is not loaded into memory until it is explicitly called by the program?
questions.explanation: Dynamic loading allows routines to be kept on disk in relocatable load format and only loaded into memory when they are needed/called.

questions.text: In the context of dynamic linking, what is a **stub**?

choices.label: A large binary file containing all library code
choices.text: A large binary file containing all library code

choices.label: A piece of code used to locate the appropriate memory-resident library routine
choices.text: A piece of code used to locate the appropriate memory-resident library routine

choices.label: A hardware register that stores the base address
choices.text: A hardware register that stores the base address

choices.label: An error log created when a library fails to load
choices.text: An error log created when a library fails to load

questions.explanation: A stub is a small piece of code included in the binary that locates the library routine or loads it if it is not already in memory.

questions.text: You have a 100MB process and a backing store with a transfer rate of 50MB/sec. If a context switch requires swapping the current process out and swapping a new 100MB process in, what is the total swapping component of the context switch time?
questions.explanation: Swap out: 100MB / 50MB/s = 2s. Swap in: 100MB / 50MB/s = 2s. Total = 4 seconds.

questions.text: Why is standard swapping typically not supported on mobile systems like iOS and Android? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: Mobile systems lack backing stores entirely
choices.text: Mobile systems lack backing stores entirely

choices.label: Flash memory has a limited number of write cycles
choices.text: Flash memory has a limited number of write cycles

choices.label: Throughput between flash memory and CPU is relatively poor
choices.text: Throughput between flash memory and CPU is relatively poor

choices.label: Mobile apps are too small to benefit from swapping
choices.text: Mobile apps are too small to benefit from swapping

questions.explanation: Mobile systems use flash memory which has limited write endurance and lower throughput compared to traditional desktop disks, making frequent swapping inefficient.

questions.text: Under **Contiguous Allocation**, where is the resident Operating System usually held?

choices.label: In high memory with the stack
choices.text: In high memory with the stack

choices.label: In low memory with the interrupt vector
choices.text: In low memory with the interrupt vector

choices.label: In the middle of the physical address space
choices.text: In the middle of the physical address space

questions.explanation: In early contiguous allocation schemes, the OS is usually held in low memory to accommodate the interrupt vector, while user processes are held in high memory.

questions.text: Which dynamic storage-allocation strategy results in the **smallest leftover hole**?
questions.explanation: Best-fit searches for the smallest hole that is still large enough, thereby producing the smallest possible leftover fragment.

questions.text: Match the following fragmentation types to their descriptions: (Select all that apply)

choices.label: External Fragmentation: Total memory space exists but is not contiguous.
choices.text: External Fragmentation: Total memory space exists but is not contiguous.

choices.label: Internal Fragmentation: Allocated memory is slightly larger than requested memory.
choices.text: Internal Fragmentation: Allocated memory is slightly larger than requested memory.

choices.label: External Fragmentation: Memory internal to a partition that is not being used.
choices.text: External Fragmentation: Memory internal to a partition that is not being used.

choices.label: Internal Fragmentation: Total memory is scattered in small holes.
choices.text: Internal Fragmentation: Total memory is scattered in small holes.

questions.explanation: External fragmentation refers to scattered holes; internal fragmentation refers to wasted space inside an allocated block.

questions.text: Which of the following is a primary advantage of **Paging**?

choices.label: It allows the physical address space of a process to be noncontiguous
choices.text: It allows the physical address space of a process to be noncontiguous

choices.label: It removes the need for a page table
choices.text: It removes the need for a page table

choices.label: It makes address translation faster than register access
choices.text: It makes address translation faster than register access

questions.explanation: Paging allows physical memory to be allocated to a process wherever it is available, meaning the physical addresses do not need to be contiguous.

questions.text: An address generated by the CPU in a paging system is divided into which two parts?
questions.explanation: Paging addresses consist of a page number (used as an index to the page table) and a page offset (the specific location within that page).

questions.text: If a system has a page size of 2,048 bytes and a process size of 72,766 bytes, how much **internal fragmentation** occurs in the last page?
questions.explanation: 72,766 / 2,048 = 35.53. 35 full pages + 1,086 bytes. The fragment is 2,048 - 1,086 = 962 bytes.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the **Translation Look-aside Buffer (TLB)**?

choices.label: To store the entire page table for every process
choices.text: To store the entire page table for every process

choices.label: To provide a fast-lookup hardware cache for page table entries
choices.text: To provide a fast-lookup hardware cache for page table entries

choices.label: To manage the transfer of pages to the backing store
choices.text: To manage the transfer of pages to the backing store

choices.label: To protect the OS from user mode access
choices.text: To protect the OS from user mode access

questions.explanation: The TLB is a high-speed associative cache that stores recently used page-to-frame mappings to avoid two memory accesses per data request.

questions.text: Calculate the Effective Access Time (EAT) given: \(\alpha\) (Hit Ratio) = 99%, TLB search time = 20ns, and Memory access time = 100ns.
questions.explanation: EAT = 0.99(20 + 100) + 0.01(20 + 100 + 100). Or following the slide formula: EAT = 0.99(100) + 0.01(200) = 99 + 2 = 101ns (assuming the 20ns is concurrent or negligible in the hit, but adding 100 for miss).

questions.text: What does a **valid-invalid bit** set to 'invalid' in a page table entry signify?

choices.label: The page is currently in the TLB
choices.text: The page is currently in the TLB

choices.label: The page is not in the process's logical address space
choices.text: The page is not in the process's logical address space

questions.explanation: The invalid bit indicates that the page is not part of the process's legal address space, resulting in a trap to the kernel if accessed.

questions.text: Which of the following describes **reentrant code**?

choices.label: Code that can be modified by multiple processes simultaneously
choices.text: Code that can be modified by multiple processes simultaneously

choices.label: Non-self-modifying (read-only) code that can be shared among processes
choices.text: Non-self-modifying (read-only) code that can be shared among processes

choices.label: Code that is loaded multiple times into different physical frames
choices.text: Code that is loaded multiple times into different physical frames

choices.label: Code that only allows one process to enter at a time
choices.text: Code that only allows one process to enter at a time

questions.explanation: Reentrant code is read-only code that never changes during execution, allowing multiple processes to share a single physical copy of the code.

questions.text: What is the primary benefit of an **Inverted Page Table**?

choices.label: It makes address translation much faster than standard paging
choices.text: It makes address translation much faster than standard paging

choices.label: It decreases the amount of memory needed to store page tables
choices.text: It decreases the amount of memory needed to store page tables

choices.label: It eliminates the need for a TLB
choices.text: It eliminates the need for a TLB

choices.label: It simplifies the implementation of shared memory
choices.text: It simplifies the implementation of shared memory

questions.explanation: Inverted page tables track physical frames rather than logical pages, significantly reducing the memory overhead for page tables in large address spaces.

questions.text: In the Intel IA-32 architecture, segments are managed using which two tables? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: IA-32 uses the GDT for segments shared among all processes and LDTs for segments private to a specific process.

questions.text: The Intel **x86-64** architecture currently implements how many bits of addressing in practice?
questions.explanation: While theoretically 64-bit, the slides note that current generation x86-64 architectures typically implement 48-bit addressing.

questions.text: The ARM architecture supports which of the following page/section sizes? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: ARM architecture is flexible, supporting 4KB and 16KB pages as well as 1MB and 16MB sections.

questions.text: Which technique involves shuffling memory contents to place all free memory together in one large block?
questions.explanation: Compaction is the solution to external fragmentation, moving processes to consolidate free space into one contiguous hole.

questions.text: In a **Segmentation** scheme, how is a logical address represented?

choices.label: As a page number and frame index
choices.text: As a page number and frame index

questions.explanation: Segmentation uses a segment number to index the segment table and an offset to find the specific location within that segment.

questions.text: Which of the following are characteristics of **Hashed Page Tables**? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: Commonly used in address spaces > 32 bits
choices.text: Commonly used in address spaces > 32 bits

choices.label: Each entry contains a chain of elements to handle collisions
choices.text: Each entry contains a chain of elements to handle collisions

choices.label: Requires a contiguous page table in memory
choices.text: Requires a contiguous page table in memory

choices.label: Clustered page tables are a variation of this technique
choices.text: Clustered page tables are a variation of this technique

questions.explanation: Hashed page tables handle sparse and large address spaces by hashing page numbers into a table that uses linked lists for collisions.
